g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of memory systems. We will discover why no single memory technology can be simultaneously fast, large, and cheap.
g.text: Speed, Size and Cost of Memory Systems

g.data-narration: A computer needs memory that is fast, large, and inexpensive. However, faster memory costs more per bit and comes in smaller capacities.
g.text: A computer requires memory that is:
No single memory technology satisfies all three!

g.data-narration: This table shows how memory technologies trade speed, size, and cost. SRAM is fastest but most expensive; storage is slowest but cheapest.
SRAM uses six transistors per bit, needs no refresh, and is very fast. DRAM uses one transistor and capacitor per bit, requires refresh, and is slower.
g.text: Access: 1-10 ns | High cost | Low density
Access: 50-100 ns | Low cost | High density

g.data-narration: The memory hierarchy organizes memory in levels. The fastest, smallest, most expensive at the top; slowest, largest, cheapest at the bottom.
Each level in the hierarchy has different characteristics. Registers are under 1 nanosecond; HDDs take milliseconds to access.
Locality makes caches effective. Temporal locality: recently accessed items are likely accessed again. Spatial locality: nearby items are accessed.
A cache hit means data was found in fast cache. A cache miss means data must be fetched from slower main memory, causing delay.
g.text: Data found in cache → Fast access
Not in cache → Fetch from memory

g.data-narration: Average Memory Access Time combines hit rate and access times. With 95% hit rate, effective speed is much closer to cache than main memory.
Average cost combines cache and memory costs weighted by size. Since main memory is much larger, average cost is close to DRAM pricing.
g.text: Since S2 >> S1 (DRAM ≫ Cache), average cost ≈ DRAM cost

g.data-narration: To summarize: memory trade-offs drive hierarchy design. Locality enables effective caching. The hierarchy gives us fast, large, affordable memory.

g.text: Speed, size, cost are inversely related

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning about memory systems. This knowledge is fundamental to understanding computer architecture and performance optimization.
